papergraph.title: Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals
papergraph.citation: Tongbo He et al. (2025) Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals doi:10.1109/ICEEPS66790.2025.11239566
papergraph.venue: 2025 4th International Conference on Energy and Electrical Power Systems (ICEEPS)
papergraph.abstract: As core equipment for grid condition monitoring, power transmission and transformation sensing terminals face critical challenges in ultra-high altitude extreme environments, including material performance degradation, abnormal electrical characteristics, and inadequate operational adaptability. Existing environmental testing standards demonstrate limitations in simulating multi-stress coupled scenarios due to insufficient simulation capabilities and outdated control strategies. This paper proposes an ultra-high altitude adaptability testing methodology for power transmission sensing terminals based on multidimensional environmental coupling loading and dynamic gradient control. Through retrofitting a comprehensive test chamber with integrated ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, and low-pressure control modules, we established a synchronized multi-environmental factor loading system. The implementation of a pressure-temperature-ultraviolet linkage algorithm coupled with a dynamic gradient control model enables precise simulation of altitude gradient variations. Experimental results demonstrate the method's effectiveness in replicating multidimensional extreme conditions: low pressure (≤70 kPa), intense ultraviolet radiation (≥1000 W/m2), wide temperature fluctuations (-40°C to 70°C), and strong winds (≥15 m/s), while significantly reducing testing cycle duration. This research provides technical support for reliability evaluation of power transmission equipment in ultra-high altitude regions such as the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, demonstrating substantial engineering application value.

papergraph.title: Research on Wireless Power Transmission Technology and Its Application in Electrical Engineering
papergraph.citation: Xiaolong Wang et al. (2025) Research on Wireless Power Transmission Technology and Its Application in Electrical Engineering doi:10.1109/ICPICS66386.2025.11347183
papergraph.venue: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Power, Intelligent Computing and Systems (ICPICS)
papergraph.abstract: In order to improve the flexibility and safety of power supply of electrical engineering systems in scenarios such as high-voltage isolation, complex environments and mobile devices, this paper studies wireless power transmission technology and its application in smart substations, comprehensively and systematically sorts out three types of wireless power supply mechanisms: electromagnetic induction, magnetic resonance coupling and microwave transmission, builds a wireless power supply system framework with magnetic resonance coupling as the core, and deploys the system in 110 kV smart substation scenarios. Test results show that the system achieves an average transmission efficiency of 78.6 % at a coupling distance of 2.8 meters, controls the power fluctuation rate within plus or minus 2.6 %, and reduces the annual interruption frequency to 3 times. The comprehensive operation stability and safety far exceed the traditional wired method. Wireless power transmission shows good engineering practice feasibility and expansion potential, and is suitable for key parts in smart grids that have high requirements for flexibility, safety and efficiency of energy supply.

papergraph.title: Consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensembles for comprehensive fault diagnosis in series-compensated ultra-high-voltage power systems
papergraph.citation: Rab Nawaz et al. (2026) Consensus-based feature selection and Bayesian-optimized stacking ensembles for comprehensive fault diagnosis in series-compensated ultra-high-voltage power systems doi:10.1038/s41598-026-49195-7
papergraph.abstract: Modern power systems incorporate ultra-high-voltage transmission lines with series compensation to enhance their reliability and stability. These configurations generate complex data patterns in electrical signals, which present challenges for accurate fault diagnosis. This complexity arises from various potential faults, such as high-impedance faults and inductive-capacitive interactions, compounded by data scarcity owing to privacy and cybersecurity constraints. This study employs a simulation-based analysis to facilitate fault detection, classification, and localization as unified Multi-Classification tasks. The methodology improves the time-frequency domain data through meta-feature engineering and utilizes consensus-based feature selection by combining gradient boosting, variance thresholding, and mutual information. Model learning is conducted using Bayesian optimization within a Stacking Ensemble, integrating Extra Trees, Light Gradient Boosting Machine, and Extreme Gradient Boosting with an optimized Random Forest meta-classifier. The evaluation considers the accuracy, computational time, inference speed, and memory usage under challenging conditions to address practical limitations. The results indicate that the Stacking Ensemble achieves the highest performance despite its greater computational demand. Extra Trees offers the fastest execution but shows decreased accuracy with increased classification complexity. The Light Gradient Boosting Machine and Extreme Gradient Boosting demonstrate optimized performance across tasks owing to enhanced data processing. These findings confirm the practical applicability of the proposed fault diagnosis framework to modern ultra-high-voltage power systems.

papergraph.title: Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects
papergraph.citation: Yuliang Jiang et al. (2026) Particle Swarm Optimization with Stagnation Counter Technique for Optimization of Main Installation Materials Cost in Power Transmission and Transformation Projects doi:10.1109/ICICACS68679.2026.11578464
papergraph.venue: 2026 4th International Conference on Integrated Circuits and Communication Systems (ICICACS)
papergraph.abstract: The cost-effective power transmission is the bulk movement of electrical energy form generating stations to substations located near demand centers. The long distance using high voltage lines and it serves as the critical bridge in the electrical grid, ensuring that energy produced as efficient, remote location reaches homes and industries. The conductors, insulators, towers and transformers contribute major portion of the total investment in power transmission and transformation projects. Selection of suitable main installation materials configurations is crucial for minimizing project costs while maintaining high transmission efficiency and system reliability. The existing optimization algorithms focus on overall project investment and faced the inefficient energy transmission problem, when the existing algorithm are unable to concentrate the multiple crucial aspects simultaneously and stuck into the local optima. To mitigate this problem, the Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm with Stagnation Counter Technique (SCT) is used proposed. The PSO is used to concentrate the multiple aspects simultaneously based on the iterative search spaces utilizing swarm collective experience recursively. The SCT is used to overcome the premature convergence problem which is occurred by the PSO, by forcing the algorithm to resume the exploration when the progress is stopped. The proposed PSO-SCT framework is achieved better results comparing to some models such as Standard Particle Swarm Optimization (SPSO), Fully Informed Particle Swarm Optimization (FIPSO). Thus, the proposed PSO-SCT based approach optimizes conductor type, conductor cross-sectional area, transformer capacity, tower configuration, and insulation selection to reduce main installation materials cost while satisfying transmission-efficiency and reliability constraints.

papergraph.title: Development of High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) Transmission Systems for Efficient Power Transfer
papergraph.citation: Naveen Garg et al. (2025) Development of High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) Transmission Systems for Efficient Power Transfer doi:10.1109/CISES66934.2025.11264962
papergraph.venue: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent and Secure Engineering Solutions (CISES)
papergraph.abstract: The direct transmission systems using the voltage of one of the largest values, HVDC, that allow transmitting electricity with high efficiency and safety over long distances may be considered one of the most important milestones in the history of electricity engineering and development of the electrical power systems. It has a few strengths such as; it has a lower power loss compared with standard alternating current (AC) systems, is more stable on the grid, and has power density. The fundamental units of the HVDC technology are transformer stations, lines and control systems. HVDC converters technology has made the major focus on voltage source converters (VSCs) and line-commutated converters (LCCs) in power conversion between AC and DC to achieve maximum efficiency of the system. Renewable energy: Here HVDC can adopt renewable energy sources like wind power and solar energy to ease transfer of power through modular and long-distance power transmission of remote generation sites to the centre of consumption. Technology HVDC is being developed in the direction of higher converter efficiency, reduced cost and decreased fault tolerance. Enhanced control algorithms and real-time monitoring software guarantee dynamic stability and easy interface with current grid infrastructures. With the information on the cleaner energy future, an even further progress of the HVDC systems that will be incorporated in the world energy transition on the way to more sustainable and resilient electricity power systems is proceeding.

papergraph.title: Research on system optimization and strategic path of China’s energy security from the perspective of electrical engineering
papergraph.citation: D. Danylchenko et al. (2025) Research on system optimization and strategic path of China’s energy security from the perspective of electrical engineering doi:10.20998/eree.2025.1(10).333342
papergraph.venue: Bulletin of the National Technical University "KhPI". Series: Energy: Reliability and Energy Efficiency
papergraph.abstract: The article presents a comprehensive study of China's energy security from a technical and economic perspective, taking into account current challenges and strategic goals of the energy transition. The main focus is on the quantitative analysis of structural problems that threaten the stability of the energy system. In particular, based on 2023 data, it was found that the structure of energy consumption remains dependent on fossil fuels: the share of coal is 56.4 %, and oil import dependence is 72.6 %. In addition, the probability of overloading the power grid in extreme weather conditions was found to be 7.3 %, indicating the system's vulnerability to climate change. The study modeled the effectiveness of the implementation of Smart Grid technologies, which increased the efficiency of ultra-high voltage power transmission by 18 %. The study also recorded a 40 % increase in the total capacity of virtual power plants and an increase in the internal rate of return of energy storage systems based on lithium batteries to 8.7 %. From the perspective of long-term strategic planning, the proposed multi-energy flow model estimates the cost of transporting 500 km of green hydrogen at 35 yuan/kg, and also forms a standardized power compensation mechanism (0.08 yuan/kW). The empirical results confirm the feasibility of such measures: the rate of rejection of new transmission lines in the northwestern regions has been reduced to 3.2 %, and the introduction of 12 GW of energy storage systems in the Yangtze Delta has reduced peak load by 15%. It is also demonstrated that the cost of hydrogen production from offshore wind energy has reached 0.3 yuan/kWh, or 35 yuan/kg, taking into account market electricity tariffs. The article proposes strategic measures to ensure energy security, including investments in scientific and technological development in the amount of 0.8 % of gross domestic product, modernization of the power grid with funding of 4.2 trillion yuan over the next 10 years, and the creation of an adaptive political monitoring system. These results form the basis for a systematic electrical engineering approach to ensuring China's energy sustainability in the context of global transformations.

papergraph.title: Technical Transformation Scheme and Practice Applied in Ultra-high Share of Renewable Energy Power System at County-level Scale
papergraph.citation: Ping Xiong et al. (2025) Technical Transformation Scheme and Practice Applied in Ultra-high Share of Renewable Energy Power System at County-level Scale doi:10.1109/ICPST65050.2025.11088518
papergraph.venue: 2025 IEEE 3rd International Conference on Power Science and Technology (ICPST)
papergraph.abstract: The rapid integration of variable renewable energy into county-level power grids has introduced significant operational challenges, particularly transmission congestion and transformer overload caused by bi-directional flow. To overcome the restriction of conventional grid reinforcement solutions focused mainly on infrastructure expansion, the work develops an integrated generation-grid-load-storage coordination framework adapted to scenarios with high penetration of renewables. The solution achieves localized energy balance through optimized energy storage allocation and operational modes control, establishing a resilient power grid architecture capable of grid-connected interaction and autonomous islanded operation. Renovation schemes of building a power system with ultra-high renewable penetration ratios are presented, in which technical routes for the coordination framework are investigated. Performance of key technologies, including grid-forming voltage source converters, smooth mode transition, and the collective control of the coordination system in support of the independent operation and stability of a 100% variable inverter-based renewable energy sources power system, is validated.

papergraph.title: Study on Ecological Environmental Impact Assessment Index System of UHV AC Transmission and Transformation Engineering-A Case Study of 1000kv UHV Transmission and Transformation Engineering from Zhangbei to Xiongan
papergraph.citation: Jiming Song et al. (2023) Study on Ecological Environmental Impact Assessment Index System of UHV AC Transmission and Transformation Engineering-A Case Study of 1000kv UHV Transmission and Transformation Engineering from Zhangbei to Xiongan doi:10.1051/e3sconf/202337002001
papergraph.abstract: Ultra-high voltage (UHV) power grid is a new transmission and transformation technology, which has the advantages of large transmission capacity, long transmission distance, low line loss, saving land resources and obvious economic benefits. Taking the Zhangbei-xiongan 1000kv AC high voltage power transmission and transformation project as an example, this paper constructs the eco-environmental index system of the UHV transmission and transformation project by using the pressure-state-response model, and constructs a set of 18 indexes eco-environmental index system, it mainly includes three aspects: the eco-environmental pressure index caused by the construction behavior in different construction stages of power transmission and transformation projects, and the eco-environmental state index given by on-the-spot investigation and study of projects, based on the construction unit on the ecological environment impact management response measures given indicators. The index system provides a basis for comprehensive and systematic evaluation of the impact of power transmission and distribution projects on the ecological environment, and finally draws the corresponding project construction evaluation conclusions. In this paper, the eco-environmental impact assessment index system of power transmission and transformation engineering has certain universality, and it has certain reference and guiding significance for the ecological environment evaluation of power transmission and transformation engineering.

papergraph.title: Study on Power Supply Reliability of Sending End Matching Power Access to Ultra-high Voltage AC and DC Hybrid Power Grid
papergraph.citation: Zheng Bowen et al. (2018) Study on Power Supply Reliability of Sending End Matching Power Access to Ultra-high Voltage AC and DC Hybrid Power Grid doi:10.1109/CICED.2018.8592243
papergraph.venue: China International Conference on Electricity Distribution
papergraph.abstract: With the continuous operation of ultra-high voltage (UHV) AC and DC power transmission and transformation projects in Inner Mongolia autonomous region, Inner Mongolia power grid has become a UHV AC and DC hybrid system. However, the development of AC and DC projects is not balanced, and the characteristics of “strong DC and weak AC” are obvious, It is bound to affect the safe and stable operation of UHV power grids, when sending end matching thermal power units connecting to UHV AC and DC hybrid power grid, the reliability of power supply is difficult to be guaranteed. Taking the sending end regional power grid of Xilinhot to Taizhou (Jiangsu Province) UHV DC transmission project (hereinafter referred to as Xitai DC) as the research object, by using Power System Analysis Software Package simulation software, this paper deeply analyzes the influence of high-power matching power supply on the safe and stable operation of the power grid after connecting to the UHV AC and DC hybrid power grid, and put forward the corresponding prevention and control measures.

papergraph.title: A Simulation Study on Grounding Faults in the Metal Shield Layer of High-Voltage Power Cables
papergraph.citation: Zifan Zhang et al. (2025) A Simulation Study on Grounding Faults in the Metal Shield Layer of High-Voltage Power Cables doi:10.1109/ICLCSE66889.2025.11389670
papergraph.venue: 2025 International Conference on Low Carbon and Smart Energy (ICLCSE)
papergraph.abstract: In the context of accelerating urbanisation and the transformation of energy structures, high voltage power cables have emerged as a pivotal transmission method within contemporary power systems. The operational reliability of these entities has been demonstrated to have a direct impact on grid security and power supply quality. Within cable structures, the metallic shielding layer fulfils a dual function: it both suppresses electromagnetic interference and protects communication facilities, while also providing a safe return path for ground faults and preventing electric shock accidents. The present study investigates the mechanisms and characteristics of short-circuit faults in cable shields. Utilising the electromagnetic transient simulation software PSCAD, a refined cable model incorporating frequency-dependent parameters was constructed. The electrical characteristics and influencing factors of such faults were revealed through analysis of the variation patterns of shield currents and zero-sequence currents under different operating conditions. The present research provides both theoretical and empirical support for the rapid diagnosis and condition assessment in engineering applications.
papergraph.scenario: grounding faults in the metal shield layer of high-voltage power cables
papergraph.contribution: theoretical and empirical support for the rapid diagnosis and condition assessment of grounding faults in high-voltage power cables
papergraph.limitation: A Simulation Study on Grounding Faults in the Metal Shield Layer of High-Voltage Power Cables In the context of accelerating urbanisation and the transformation of energy structures, high voltage power cables have emerged as a pivotal transmission method within contemporary power systems.

papergraph.title: Development and Experimental Study of De-Icing Short-Circuit Device for Ultra-High Voltage Transmission Lines Based on Rapid Clamping Structure
papergraph.citation: Jinpei Wang et al. (2026) Development and Experimental Study of De-Icing Short-Circuit Device for Ultra-High Voltage Transmission Lines Based on Rapid Clamping Structure doi:10.1109/ICSGGE69348.2026.11508193
papergraph.venue: 2026 5th International Conference on Smart Grid and Green Energy (ICSGGE)
papergraph.abstract: To address the issues of inconvenient installation and poor adaptability associated with traditional fixed crimping drainage lines during sectionalized direct-current (DC) icemelting of transmission lines, this paper aims to develop a novel phase-to-phase short-circuit device for conductor ice-melting. The device adopts a “T-shaped” aluminum alloy body and a modular quick-press cover plate. It significantly enhances heat dissipation performance by utilizing the reserved air convection gaps. Through a highly reliable hinge and a quick-pressing mechanism, it achieves non-pressurized quick assembly and disassembly and stable connection with the wires. Experimental results demonstrate that under the rated current required by engineering standards, the steady-state temperature rise of the device is lower than that of the conductor. Furthermore, the ratio of its DC resistance to that of an equal-length conductor is less than 1.1, satisfying standard requirements. Characterized by its lightweight structure, convenient installation, and reliable current-carrying capability, the device flexibly adapts to various bundle conductors and two-phase/three-phase short-circuiting scenarios. It significantly enhances the efficiency and safety of emergency icemelting operations, providing effective technical equipment for the power grid to mitigate icing hazards.

papergraph.title: The Seismic Response of Ultra-High Voltage Gas-Insulated Transmission Lines
papergraph.citation: Wei Lv et al. (2023) The Seismic Response of Ultra-High Voltage Gas-Insulated Transmission Lines doi:10.1088/1742-6596/2557/1/012065
papergraph.abstract: As an important part of the substation, the seismic capacity of ultra-high voltage (UHV) electrical equipment is related to the safe and stable operation of the substation and the whole power grid. The UHV gas-insulated transmission lines (GIL) have many structural characteristics, such as long pipeline distance, the large span between supports and the obvious amplification effect of supports. Its natural frequency is close to the predominant frequency of the seismic wave, resulting in its susceptibility to resonance or resonance-like and high seismic susceptibility. In order to analyze the seismic performance of UHV GIL better, the vibration mode decomposition response spectrum method and the dynamic time-history analysis method are used to analyze the seismic response of UHV GIL in this paper. The search for seismic weak positions of GIL is a reference for effectively improving the seismic performance of UHV GIL.

papergraph.title: Overview of Main Electrical Equipment for UHVDC Transmission
papergraph.citation: Xiangyu Meng (2025) Overview of Main Electrical Equipment for UHVDC Transmission doi:10.5220/0014320200004918
papergraph.venue: Proceedings of the 3rd International Conference on Computer Science and Mechatronics
papergraph.abstract: Ultra-high Voltage Direct Current (UHVDC) transmission is the mainstream solution of modern remote power transmission. Converter from the 12 pulse converter mainly using thyristors to the series capacitor converter, and finally developed to the converter using fully controlled devices. The former uses thyristor devices, and transmission-line system of electric power provide the commutation voltage to complete the commutation, the latter is composed of fully controlled devices, and the commutation is completed by the self-turn-off characteristics of the devices. Transmission line protection is mainly divided into traveling wave protection, differential undervoltage protection and longitudinal differential protection. This study conducts a comparative evaluation and systematic categorization of the principal protection methodologies utilized in transmission line systems, with a focus on delineating their respective merits, limitations, and distinguishing operational characteristics under varying fault scenarios. This paper introduces the converter and transmission protection technology from the aspects of circuit structure, principle, advantages and disadvantages. The final section of this research synthesizes key findings while delineating emerging trajectories for next-generation ultra-high-voltage (UHV) power transmission systems, with particular emphasis on their transformative potential in enabling large-scale renewable energy integration and transcontinental grid interoperability.

papergraph.title: Analysis and Evaluation Methods for UHV AC Retrofitting Schemes and Application Effects under Renewable Energy Transmission Scenarios
papergraph.citation: H. Cai et al. (2026) Analysis and Evaluation Methods for UHV AC Retrofitting Schemes and Application Effects under Renewable Energy Transmission Scenarios doi:10.32604/ee.2026.081805
papergraph.abstract: Aiming at the challenge of large-scale centralized integration of high-proportion renewable energy, and addressing current bottlenecks in ultra-high voltage (UHV) AC power grids—such as low utilization rates of transmission corridors and poor active power flow controllability—this paper proposes various upgrading and transformation schemes for UHV AC ring networks. These schemes aim to simultaneously enhance the renewable energy accommodation and operational regulation capabilities of the AC grid. First, targeting typical UHV double-circuit transmission corridors, three topological schemes are designed: direct AC integration of renewable energy, single-circuit voltage source converter-based high voltage direct current (VSC-HVDC) transformation, and double-circuit full VSC-HVDC transformation. Second, a unified DC power flow mathematical model is established to theoretically derive and reveal the power flow control characteristics under traditional AC integration, single-circuit VSC-HVDC transformation, and full VSC-HVDC transformation. Finally, a multi-dimensional evaluation system is constructed, encompassing power flow control flexibility, static and transient voltage stability, and short-circuit current levels. Simulations and analyses are then conducted using the Power System Dispatcher-Based Performance Analysis (PSD-BPA) program based on the 2040 planning data for the East China Power Grid. The research results indicate that the double-circuit full VSC-HVDC transformation scheme can overcome the physical constraints of the AC grid’s natural impedance, achieving active regulation of transmission power. Simultaneously, it effectively limits short-circuit currents by leveraging its nonlinear high-impedance characteristics. This scheme serves as an effective technical pathway for resolving the challenges of high-proportion renewable energy transmission and ensuring the safe and stable operation of receiving-end power grids.

papergraph.title: Modeling and Enhancement of Seismic Resilience for Ultra High Voltage Converter Station
papergraph.citation: Zhihang Xue et al. (2024) Modeling and Enhancement of Seismic Resilience for Ultra High Voltage Converter Station doi:10.1109/ICPEE64457.2024.10949981
papergraph.venue: 2024 8th International Conference on Power and Energy Engineering (ICPEE)
papergraph.abstract: The power system is a critical infrastructure for the development of the national economy, and earthquakes pose a significant threat to the operational safety of the power grid. As a key node for long-distance electrical power transmission, the ability of ultra-high voltage (UHV) converter stations to withstand earthquake and recover swiftly afterward has garnered considerable attention within the industry. This article focuses on the modeling and enhancement methods for the seismic resistance of UHV converter station. Firstly, a component diagram of the UHV converter stations is established, and the post-earthquake transmission function index for individual station is defined. Subsequently, based on seismic intensity data, the failure probability of equipment within each branch at a specified seismic intensity is calculated. Additionally, the seismic resilience index R of the UHV converter station is defined, considering the post-earthquake recovery process. Finally, using data from a specific ±800 kV UHV converter station as a case study, its seismic resilience is assessed. Through comparative analysis, methods to enhance seismic resilience are explored. The research holds significant guidance for enhancing the seismic resilience of the UHV voltage converter stations.
papergraph.method: seismic resilience index R and failure probability calculation
papergraph.contribution: modeling and enhancement methods for the seismic resistance of UHV converter stations
papergraph.limitation: As a key node for long-distance electrical power transmission, the ability of ultra-high voltage (UHV) converter stations to withstand earthquake and recover swiftly afterward has garnered considerable attention within the industry.

keynotes.title: Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals

keynote.title: Testing Methods for Ultra-High Altitude Environmental Adaptability of Power Transmission and Transformation Sensing Terminals
keynote.core_problem: Power transmission and transformation sensing terminals face critical challenges in ultra-high altitude extreme environments, such as material performance degradation, abnormal electrical characteristics, and inadequate operational adaptability. Existing testing standards are limited in simulating multi-stress coupled scenarios due to insufficient simulation capabilities and outdated control strategies.
contributions: Proposed an ultra-high altitude adaptability testing methodology based on multidimensional environmental coupling loading and dynamic gradient control.

Retrofitted a comprehensive test chamber with integrated ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, and low-pressure control modules to establish a synchronized multi-environmental factor loading system.
Implemented a pressure-temperature-ultraviolet linkage algorithm coupled with a dynamic gradient control model for precise simulation of altitude gradient variations.
methods: Retrofitting a test chamber with specific environmental control modules (ultraviolet irradiation, air circulation, low-pressure control).
Development and implementation of a pressure-temperature-ultraviolet linkage algorithm and a dynamic gradient control model.
experiments_or_evidence: Experimental results demonstrated the method's effectiveness in replicating multidimensional extreme conditions: low pressure (≤70 kPa), intense ultraviolet radiation (≥1000 W/m²), wide temperature fluctuations (-40°C to 70°C), and strong winds (≥15 m/s).
Significantly reduced testing cycle duration.
assumptions: The proposed testing methodology can accurately simulate the multi-stress coupled scenarios found in ultra-high altitude environments.
The retrofitted test chamber and implemented algorithms effectively replicate the environmental conditions of ultra-high altitudes.

gap_signals.text: This research provides technical support for reliability evaluation of power transmission equipment in ultra-high altitude regions, demonstrating substantial engineering application value.

important_claims.claim: The proposed testing methodology effectively replicates multidimensional extreme conditions found in ultra-high altitude environments.
important_claims.evidence: Experimental results show the method's capability to simulate low pressure, intense ultraviolet radiation, wide temperature fluctuations, and strong winds, while also reducing testing cycle duration.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The methodology and experimental setup can be used by other researchers and engineers to evaluate and improve the reliability of power transmission and transformation sensing terminals in ultra-high altitude regions.

keynote.core_problem: Improving the flexibility, safety, and efficiency of power supply in electrical engineering systems, especially in challenging environments like high-voltage isolation, complex terrains, and for mobile devices.
contributions: Comprehensive and systematic review of three types of wireless power supply mechanisms: electromagnetic induction, magnetic resonance coupling, and microwave transmission.
Development and deployment of a wireless power supply system using magnetic resonance coupling in a 110 kV smart substation.
methods: Review and analysis of existing wireless power transmission technologies.
Design and implementation of a magnetic resonance coupling-based wireless power supply system.
Testing and evaluation of the system's performance in a 110 kV smart substation environment.
experiments_or_evidence: The system achieved an average transmission efficiency of 78.6% at a coupling distance of 2.8 meters.
Controlled the power fluctuation rate within ±2.6%.
Reduced the annual interruption frequency to 3 times, indicating improved stability and safety over traditional wired methods.
assumptions: Wireless power transmission can provide better flexibility, safety, and efficiency compared to traditional wired methods in specific applications such as smart substations.

gap_signals.text: No explicit limitations or future work are mentioned, but the focus on magnetic resonance coupling suggests potential exploration into other wireless power transmission methods or further optimization of the current system.

datasets_or_materials: 110 kV smart substation as the test environment.
code_or_implementation: Implementation details of the magnetic resonance coupling-based wireless power supply system.

important_claims.claim: The wireless power supply system achieves higher operational stability and safety compared to traditional wired methods.
important_claims.evidence: Test results show an average transmission efficiency of 78.6%, a power fluctuation rate controlled within ±2.6%, and an annual interruption frequency reduced to 3 times.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides a practical example of deploying wireless power transmission technology in a real-world setting, which can serve as a reference for future research and development in enhancing energy supply flexibility, safety, and efficiency in smart grids and other critical infrastructure.

important_claims.evidence: Performance of key technologies, including grid-forming voltage source converters, smooth mode transition, and the collective control of the coordination system, is validated.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The integrated generation-grid-load-storage coordination framework and the technical routes for the coordination framework can be used as a reference for developing resilient power systems with high renewable energy penetration. The validation of key technologies provides a basis for further research and implementation in other regions or larger scales.

keynotes.title: Study on Ecological Environmental Impact Assessment Index System of UHV AC Transmission and Transformation Engineering-A Case Study of 1000kv UHV Transmission and Transformation Engineering from Zhangbei to Xiongan

keynote.title: Study on Ecological Environmental Impact Assessment Index System of UHV AC Transmission and Transformation Engineering-A Case Study of 1000kv UHV Transmission and Transformation Engineering from Zhangbei to Xiongan
keynote.core_problem: Developing a comprehensive and systematic eco-environmental impact assessment index system for UHV transmission and transformation projects.
contributions: Constructed an eco-environmental index system using the pressure-state-response model for UHV transmission and transformation projects.
Provided a set of 18 indexes that cover ecological pressure, state, and response measures.
methods: Pressure-state-response (PSR) model for constructing the index system
On-the-spot investigation and study of the project
experiments_or_evidence: Case study of the 1000kv UHV transmission and transformation project from Zhangbei to Xiongan

gap_signals.text: The eco-environmental impact assessment index system has certain universality and can be used as a reference for other power transmission and transformation engineering projects.

important_claims.claim: The constructed eco-environmental index system provides a basis for comprehensive and systematic evaluation of the impact of power transmission and distribution projects on the ecological environment.
important_claims.evidence: The index system is based on the pressure-state-response model and includes 18 specific indexes covering various aspects of the ecological impact.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The developed index system and the methodology can be reused or adapted for evaluating the ecological impacts of other UHV or high-voltage transmission and transformation projects, providing a structured approach for environmental assessment.

keynotes.title: Study on Power Supply Reliability of Sending End Matching Power Access to Ultra-high Voltage AC and DC Hybrid Power Grid

keynote.title: Study on Power Supply Reliability of Sending End Matching Power Access to Ultra-high Voltage AC and DC Hybrid Power Grid
keynote.core_problem: The imbalance in the development of AC and DC projects, characterized by a 'strong DC and weak AC' system, affects the safe and stable operation of UHV power grids, particularly when thermal power units are connected, making it difficult to ensure power supply reliability.
contributions: Analyzed the impact of high-power matching power supply on the safe and stable operation of the UHV AC and DC hybrid power grid
Proposed corresponding prevention and control measures to improve power supply reliability
methods: Used Power System Analysis Software Package for simulation
experiments_or_evidence: Simulated the influence of high-power matching power supply on the Xitai DC project's grid stability

gap_signals.text: Further research is needed to explore the long-term effects and additional measures to enhance the stability of UHV AC and DC hybrid systems.

datasets_or_materials: Xilinhot to Taizhou (Jiangsu Province) UHV DC transmission project data

important_claims.claim: The connection of high-power matching power supplies to UHV AC and DC hybrid grids can significantly affect the grid's safe and stable operation.
important_claims.evidence: Simulation results from the Xitai DC project using the Power System Analysis Software Package

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The study provides a framework for analyzing and improving the reliability of power supply in UHV AC and DC hybrid systems, which can be adapted to other similar power grid configurations.

keynotes.title: A Simulation Study on Grounding Faults in the Metal Shield Layer of High-Voltage Power Cables

keynote.title: A Simulation Study on Grounding Faults in the Metal Shield Layer of High-Voltage Power Cables
keynote.core_problem: Investigating the mechanisms and characteristics of short-circuit faults in the metallic shielding layer of high-voltage power cables to improve grid security and power supply quality.
contributions: Constructed a refined cable model incorporating frequency-dependent parameters using PSCAD.
Analyzed the electrical characteristics and influencing factors of grounding faults in cable shields.
Provided theoretical and empirical support for rapid diagnosis and condition assessment of grounding faults.
methods: Utilized electromagnetic transient simulation software (PSCAD) to construct a refined cable model.
Analyzed the variation patterns of shield currents and zero-sequence currents under different operating conditions.
experiments_or_evidence: Simulated and analyzed the behavior of shield currents and zero-sequence currents during grounding faults.
assumptions: The cable model accurately represents the real-world behavior of high-voltage power cables.
Frequency-dependent parameters are critical for accurate simulation of fault conditions.

gap_signals.text: Further research is needed to apply the findings in real-world power systems for practical validation.
The study could be extended to include more diverse and complex fault scenarios to further validate the model.

datasets_or_materials: Electromagnetic transient simulation data from PSCAD

important_claims.claim: The metallic shielding layer in high-voltage power cables plays a crucial role in suppressing electromagnetic interference, protecting communication facilities, and providing a safe return path for ground faults.
important_claims.evidence: The operational reliability of these entities has been demonstrated to have a direct impact on grid security and power supply quality.

important_claims.claim: The refined cable model with frequency-dependent parameters provides a more accurate representation of grounding faults.
important_claims.evidence: The analysis of shield currents and zero-sequence currents under different operating conditions revealed the electrical characteristics and influencing factors of such faults.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The refined cable model and simulation results can be used by other researchers to further investigate grounding faults in high-voltage power cables and to develop more robust diagnostic and monitoring systems.

keynotes.title: Development and Experimental Study of De-Icing Short-Circuit Device for Ultra-High Voltage Transmission Lines Based on Rapid Clamping Structure

keynote.title: Development and Experimental Study of De-Icing Short-Circuit Device for Ultra-High Voltage Transmission Lines Based on Rapid Clamping Structure
keynote.core_problem: Inconvenient installation and poor adaptability of traditional fixed crimping drainage lines during sectionalized direct-current (DC) icemelting of transmission lines.
contributions: Development of a novel phase-to-phase short-circuit device for conductor ice-melting with a 'T-shaped' aluminum alloy body and modular quick-press cover plate.
Enhanced heat dissipation through reserved air convection gaps.
Achieved non-pressurized quick assembly and disassembly, and stable connection with wires using a highly reliable hinge and quick-pressing mechanism.
methods: Design and fabrication of the T-shaped aluminum alloy body and modular quick-press cover plate.
Implementation of a highly reliable hinge and quick-pressing mechanism for easy installation and removal.
Utilization of air convection gaps to enhance heat dissipation.
experiments_or_evidence: Experimental results showing that under the rated current required by engineering standards, the steady-state temperature rise of the device is lower than that of the conductor.
The ratio of the device's DC resistance to that of an equal-length conductor is less than 1.1, meeting standard requirements.
assumptions: The device will be used in ultra-high voltage transmission line de-icing operations.
The device will be installed and operated according to the specified engineering standards.

gap_signals.text: The device significantly enhances the efficiency and safety of emergency icemelting operations, providing effective technical equipment for the power grid to mitigate icing hazards.

important_claims.claim: The steady-state temperature rise of the device is lower than that of the conductor under the rated current.
important_claims.evidence: Experimental results demonstrate that under the rated current required by engineering standards, the steady-state temperature rise of the device is lower than that of the conductor.

important_claims.claim: The ratio of the device's DC resistance to that of an equal-length conductor is less than 1.1, satisfying standard requirements.
important_claims.evidence: Experimental results show that the ratio of its DC resistance to that of an equal-length conductor is less than 1.1, meeting standard requirements.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The design and experimental validation of the de-icing short-circuit device can serve as a reference for future research on improving the efficiency and safety of de-icing operations in ultra-high voltage transmission lines.

keynotes.title: The Seismic Response of Ultra-High Voltage Gas-Insulated Transmission Lines

keynote.title: The Seismic Response of Ultra-High Voltage Gas-Insulated Transmission Lines
keynote.core_problem: Analyzing the seismic performance and identifying weak positions in ultra-high voltage (UHV) gas-insulated transmission lines (GIL) to improve their seismic capacity.
contributions: Application of vibration mode decomposition response spectrum method and dynamic time-history analysis method for analyzing the seismic response of UHV GIL.
methods: Vibration mode decomposition response spectrum method
Dynamic time-history analysis method

gap_signals.text: The need for advanced methods to analyze the seismic response of UHV GIL, indicating a gap in current methodologies.

important_claims.claim: UHV GIL is susceptible to resonance or resonance-like effects due to its natural frequency being close to the predominant frequency of seismic waves.
important_claims.evidence: Structural characteristics such as long pipeline distance, large span between supports, and the amplification effect of supports contribute to this susceptibility.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The methods and findings can be used by researchers and engineers to enhance the seismic design and safety of UHV GIL systems, potentially leading to more resilient power grid infrastructure.

keynotes.title: Overview of Main Electrical Equipment for UHVDC Transmission

keynote.title: Overview of Main Electrical Equipment for UHVDC Transmission
keynote.core_problem: Evaluating and categorizing the principal protection methodologies in UHVDC transmission systems, focusing on their merits, limitations, and operational characteristics under varying fault scenarios.
contributions: Conducts a comparative evaluation and systematic categorization of the principal protection methodologies utilized in transmission line systems
Introduces converter and transmission protection technology from the aspects of circuit structure, principle, advantages, and disadvantages
Synthesizes key findings and discusses emerging trajectories for next-generation UHV power transmission systems

gap_signals.text: Transformative potential in enabling large-scale renewable energy integration and transcontinental grid interoperability

important_claims.claim: UHVDC transmission is the mainstream solution for modern remote power transmission
important_claims.evidence: The paper focuses on UHVDC as a critical technology for long-distance power transfer.

important_claims.claim: Protection methodologies in UHVDC transmission have distinct operational characteristics under varying fault scenarios
important_claims.evidence: The study evaluates and categorizes these methodologies to highlight their respective merits and limitations.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This paper provides a comprehensive overview and comparison of UHVDC transmission protection technologies, which can be valuable for researchers and engineers working on improving and developing new UHVDC systems, especially in the context of integrating renewable energy sources and enhancing grid interoperability.

keynotes.title: Analysis and Evaluation Methods for UHV AC Retrofitting Schemes and Application Effects under Renewable Energy Transmission Scenarios

keynote.title: Analysis and Evaluation Methods for UHV AC Retrofitting Schemes and Application Effects under Renewable Energy Transmission Scenarios
keynote.core_problem: The challenge of large-scale centralized integration of high-proportion renewable energy in ultra-high voltage (UHV) AC power grids, including low utilization rates of transmission corridors and poor active power flow controllability.
contributions: Proposing various upgrading and transformation schemes for UHV AC ring networks to enhance the renewable energy accommodation and operational regulation capabilities of the AC grid.
Designing three topological schemes: direct AC integration of renewable energy, single-circuit VSC-HVDC transformation, and double-circuit full VSC-HVDC transformation.
Establishing a unified DC power flow mathematical model to derive and reveal power flow control characteristics under different integration and transformation scenarios.
Constructing a multi-dimensional evaluation system to assess power flow control flexibility, static and transient voltage stability, and short-circuit current levels.
methods: Designing and comparing three topological schemes for UHV AC ring networks.
Developing a unified DC power flow mathematical model.
Simulating and analyzing the proposed schemes using the Power System Dispatcher-Based Performance Analysis (PSD-BPA) program with 2040 planning data for the East China Power Grid.
experiments_or_evidence: Simulations and analyses conducted using the PSD-BPA program based on 2040 planning data for the East China Power Grid.

gap_signals.text: Further research is needed to explore the practical implementation and long-term effects of the proposed retrofitting schemes.

datasets_or_materials: 2040 planning data for the East China Power Grid
code_or_implementation: Power System Dispatcher-Based Performance Analysis (PSD-BPA) program

important_claims.claim: The double-circuit full VSC-HVDC transformation scheme can overcome the physical constraints of the AC grid’s natural impedance, achieving active regulation of transmission power.
important_claims.evidence: Simulations and analyses using the PSD-BPA program based on 2040 planning data for the East China Power Grid.

important_claims.claim: The double-circuit full VSC-HVDC transformation scheme effectively limits short-circuit currents by leveraging its nonlinear high-impedance characteristics.
important_claims.evidence: Simulations and analyses using the PSD-BPA program based on 2040 planning data for the East China Power Grid.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The proposed retrofitting schemes and the established evaluation system provide a framework for enhancing the integration and operational regulation of renewable energy in UHV AC power grids, which can be adapted and applied to other regions and scenarios.

keynotes.title: Modeling and Enhancement of Seismic Resilience for Ultra High Voltage Converter Station

keynote.title: Modeling and Enhancement of Seismic Resilience for Ultra High Voltage Converter Station
keynote.core_problem: The vulnerability of ultra-high voltage (UHV) converter stations to earthquakes and the need to enhance their seismic resilience to ensure the operational safety of the power grid.
contributions: Established a component diagram of UHV converter stations and defined a post-earthquake transmission function index.
Calculated the failure probability of equipment within each branch at a specified seismic intensity.
Defined the seismic resilience index R, considering the post-earthquake recovery process.
Assessed the seismic resilience of a specific ±800 kV UHV converter station using real data.
Explored methods to enhance seismic resilience through comparative analysis.
methods: Component diagram establishment for UHV converter stations.
Calculation of equipment failure probability based on seismic intensity data.
Definition and application of the seismic resilience index R.
Case study assessment of a ±800 kV UHV converter station.
experiments_or_evidence: Seismic resilience assessment of a specific ±800 kV UHV converter station.
assumptions: The provided seismic intensity data is accurate and representative.
The failure probability calculations are based on reliable historical and experimental data.

gap_signals.text: Further research and practical applications are needed to validate and refine the proposed methods for enhancing seismic resilience in UHV converter stations.

datasets_or_materials: Data from a specific ±800 kV UHV converter station.

important_claims.claim: The defined seismic resilience index R provides a comprehensive measure of the UHV converter station's ability to withstand and recover from an earthquake.
important_claims.evidence: The index R was applied to a case study, and its effectiveness in assessing and guiding the enhancement of seismic resilience was demonstrated.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The modeling and assessment methods, along with the defined seismic resilience index, can be adapted and applied to other critical infrastructure to enhance their seismic resilience. The case study provides a practical example that can guide future research and implementation in the field of seismic engineering for power systems.
